text: The surgeon positions all four robotic arms through the seventh intercostal space, with the most posterior arm placed approximately four centimeters from the spine.
The robotic arms are positioned with a nine-centimeter distance between them, and the most anterior arm features a twelve-millimeter port designed to accommodate a robotic stapler.
A 15mm assistant port is placed in the intercostal space immediately above the diaphragm, visible as part of the robotic setup for the esophagectomy procedure.
The surgeon begins the procedure by dividing the pulmonary ligament, using robotic instruments to carefully separate the ligament from surrounding structures.
The surgeon uses arm one of the robotic system to retract the right lower lobe of the lung forward and upward, providing better access to the surgical field.
The surgeon identifies and carefully protects the inferior pulmonary vein, ensuring it remains intact during the procedure.
Arm one uses a gauze rolled like a cigar to retract the right lower lobe forward, enhancing exposure of the surgical field.
The surgeon performs a maneuver to facilitate the dissection of the posterior mediastinal pleura, likely using retraction techniques to expose the pleura for further surgical intervention.
The surgeon excises the subcarinal lymph nodes and mediastinal lymph node station seven, removing these structures from the surrounding tissue in the mediastinum.
The surgeon encounters large bronchial arteries surrounding the lymph nodes, which are cauterized to prevent bleeding during the dissection.
The surgical field is kept clear to facilitate the identification of the vagus nerve and esophagus, minimizing the risk of injury to these structures.
The surgeon dissects the sump node located between the right upper lobe bronchus and the bronchus intermedius, preparing the area for the future completion of the posterior oblique fissure.
The surgeon identifies and dissects around the medial basilar pulmonary artery branch, using it as a landmark before proceeding to complete the anterior oblique fissure.
The surgeon identifies and carefully protects the right middle lobe bronchus, ensuring it is not damaged during the procedure.
The surgeon uses a vessel loop to retract surrounding tissues, providing clear exposure of the surgical field.
The surgeon positions a robotic green load, 30mm stapler at an optimal angle for stapling, ensuring precise alignment with the target tissue.
The surgeon dissects the pulmonary artery within the fissure, carefully separating it from surrounding tissue structures.
The surgeon identifies and protects the superior segmental arteries, ensuring these vessels are preserved during the procedure.
The surgeon uses the tip-up instrument in arm number one to roll the lung forward, delivering a vessel loop to create a clear window for the robotic stapler.
The surgeon divides the posterior oblique fissure using a green load robotic stapler, completing the separation of the tissue within the fissure.
The surgeon identifies and carefully protects the right middle lobe artery, ensuring it is preserved during the procedure.
The surgeon circumferentially dissects the ongoing pulmonary artery for the right lower lobe, carefully freeing it from surrounding tissue within the fissure.
The surgeon uses blunt instruments to create a visible window around the vessels that are to be divided, ensuring clear access for subsequent steps.
The surgeon encircles the ongoing pulmonary artery with a vessel loop and divides it using a wide load, thirty millimeter robotic stapler, after creating a comfortable window around the vessel.
The surgeon carefully positions the stapler to ensure the staple line does not occlude blood flow into the right middle lobe artery, maintaining its patency during the procedure.

The surgeon positions the cardiac instrument from robotic arm number four between the inferior and superior pulmonary veins, while the TPAP instrument from arm number one rolls the lung forward, revealing the anatomical structures for further dissection.
The surgeon circumferentially dissects the inferior pulmonary vein, encircles it with a vessel loop, and divides it using a wide load, thirty millimeter robotic stapler.
The surgeon identifies the right middle lobe bronchus and ensures its protection during the procedure.
The surgeon divides the right lower lobe bronchus using a green load, thirty millimeter robotic stapler, completing the separation of this bronchial segment.
The surgeon gently inflates the lung before firing the stapler to ensure the staple line does not occlude airflow into the right middle lobe bronchus.
The surgeon removes the specimen using a thoracoscopic bag, ensuring it is securely contained for extraction from the thoracic cavity.
The surgeon completes the dissection of mediastinal lymph nodes, focusing on the remaining sub nodes within the surgical field.
The surgeon uses a bipolar dissector to perform fine dissection around the leaf nodes, carefully manipulating the tissue with precision.
The surgeon removes all visible subcarinal lymph nodes, ensuring complete dissection of these structures from the surrounding tissue.
The surgeon applies Surgicel to a node for hemostasis, visibly controlling bleeding in the surgical field.
The surgeon dissects the right lower paratracheal leaf nodes at station IV-R, carefully separating them from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon uses arm number two to carefully control retraction during the dissection of leaf nodes, ensuring precise manipulation of the tissue.
The surgeon uses a finger of a sterile glove to remove large, unfriable leaf nodes from the surgical site.
The surgeon injects Xperel into multiple intercostal spaces using a 25-gauge butterfly needle, likely for local anesthesia or pain management.
